3	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Financial
Unique Mechanism
EXAMPLE
Financial (Book + Newsletter)
(00:01)
All right, so we’ll do another example of a unique mechanism. This one is
coming from a financial offer, a book plus newsletter deal. It’s from George
Gilder. I’m actually going to grab the link here and pop it in, that way I can
show you it real quick. This is an Agora run offer with George Gilder, who was
the treasury secretary or financial advisor -He did something with Reagan,
I’m sure it says it here. He was a part of Ronald Reagan’s cabinet and it’s got
lots of credibility, but the offer is to get a free copy of his book and to also be
enrolled in his newsletter when you do it. He’s a cool guy, a ton of credibility.
The book is called, “Life After Google”. He wants you, basically right here,
you get copy of the book and you’re going to subscribe to his newsletter, the
Gilder report.
(01:00)
So, that’s what we’ve got going on now. Now it’s a long letter, so rather than
read through the entire thing, and again, I will share this link with you. Let me
show you, I went through and kind of pulled out what the mechanism behind
the problem solution looks like. I’m going to walk you through that right now.
I’m just going to read through most of this for you. You’ll notice it’s a little
bit long, It’s not crazy though, but part of it too we can kind of skim or skip
over and I’ll show you in a moment why. All right. So every one of our biggest
companies today, Apple, Facebook, Microsoft, Amazon, and of course, Google,
is a tech Titan that’s built their empire on what most of us know as big data.
(01:46)
Funny, they have a typo there cause I copy and pasted this. Both their empire
and what most of us know as big data, that is, they feed off the information we
give them. They give away their services in exchange for advertising dollars.
They give you stuff, and you give them data, and then they sell ads. Think about
it. You give Facebook a list of your friends, your travel plans, your opinions
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 11 --

4	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Financial
and interests. You give Google your shopping plans, your iPhone tracks your
location literally to the last fraction of a mile. What you get in return can be
amazing. Ease of travel, ease of communication, ease of search, and even free
search besides. Here will be the fatal fall that dooms Google and every other
business built on those same data thirsty foundations. Why? Don’t get me
wrong, each one of these giants are among the most important companies in
financial history and each has replaced the biggest corporations of 10 years
earlier, Exxon, Walmart, China, petroleum and others.
(02:39)
But just as those firms are cycled out of the top positions on wall street and
across our economy, so will these tech giants be forced into retreat, thanks
to the very same phenomenon that once helped them explode in size. As I
write on page 90 of Life After Google, the most important effect of the Google
model is that you avoid the real time demands of preventing hacks, and thefts.
Google can place the burden of security on the customer. This very lack of
concern of security will be Google’s undoing. In other words, our new big data
driven universe has a fundamental flaw. As it stands, literally billions of items
of personal data is hacked every year. The Google model cannot last. It’s
destined to crumble and with that we’ll lose a major cornerstone of today’s
massive digital economy. As I said, one thing for sure is that 10 years from
now, big tech tech companies that dominate our world will not be the same
companies you hear about and read about so much today. Who will take their
place?
(03:31)
You see, today’s internet wasn’t built for protecting your credit card information,
guarding your online banking transactions or shielding your family photos from
prying eyes. It was built for openly sharing information like a giant, gigantic
encyclopedia. This makes it deeply unsafe and insecure by design. How many
hacks and how many billions of data breaches will it take before we admit that?
Scan your memory and ask yourself, have you ever bought a bag of topsoil or
a lawnmower at Home Depot? Because if you have, you need to know that
hackers broke into their computers in September, 2014 and sold 56 million
credit card numbers, hopefully not yours. Meanwhile, have you or anyone you
know ever taken an Uber? Because in 2016 two hackers stole names, emails,
and mobile numbers for as many as 57 million of their customers. Again, I
hope you weren’t one of them. And do you have health insurance?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 11 --

5	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Financial
(04:17)
Chances are you do, in which case you should know that Anthem, America’s
second biggest insurer suffered a major security breach in 2015. Foreign
hackers broke into Anthem’s database and swipe names, social security
numbers and even job histories for close to 79 million customers. And the list
goes on. And they list a couple more examples. Sony PlayStation, Marriott,
Equifax. But that still does nothing to guarantee that your data is safe. If you
haven’t been exposed already, how long before you are? And how long before
wall streets other biggest companies get hit with similar fines? How long
before hackers go after an even bigger price? Say a trillion dollar heist from
America’s banking system? After all, the Swift system for transferring money
overseas has already been hacked. And how long before you wake up to find
your assets frozen or to news that some hacker in Russia or Singapore has
used your name to take out a new mortgage, opened a bank account, or get
a line of credit? Or some military hackers have bought down crucial parts of
our power grid.
(05:12)
And get this, as an American, you’re especially vulnerable because 97% of all
records stolen originate in the United States. Okay, so that’s the problem. The
problem is that the architecture of today’s internet is fundamentally unsecure.
And, because Google and Facebook and Apple and all these people run on that
architecture, they are fundamentally insecure too. There’s an inherent flaw, and
I should’ve said this at the beginning, but the book and the newsletter again,
this is financial, so it really is selling investment opportunities, opportunities
to get rich, opportunities to make more money for retirement, things like that,
and so the argument here is that, you know, because of this problem that
the fundamental kind of underlying nature of today’s internet is insecure and
unsafe, that the stocks of today are going to decline, but there will be new
opportunities, to get new stocks and new companies are going to take their
place.
(06:11)
You kind of saw that with the companies that are the top today weren’t the top
10 years ago. And, whether selling you on is the idea of the companies that
are going to become the top ones 10 years from today, which are different,
then you can invest in those now. And that’s sort of the big selling or value
proposition from this letter. So, as you’ll see here, and that’s what we’re talking
about the mechanism of a solution, which as you’ll note is really not that long,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 11 --

6	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Financial
but it works. All right, but it doesn’t have to be that way. As I say on page 92 of
my book, some thousands of companies you’ve never heard of are investing
billions in that effort to fix the lack of security on the internet. Collectively, they
will give birth to a new network whose most powerful architectural imperative
will be security of transactions.
(06:52)
So fundamental will security be to this new system that its very name will be
derived from it. It will be the crypto chasm. If that’s the first time you’re hearing
the term cryptocosm, I’m not surprised. The cryptocosm is what I call this new
system of the world. As you can guess, it gets its roots in the cryptographic
revolution that gave us Bitcoin, but it’s so much more. As I reveal on page 267,
Bitcoin, aimed to burst the over-stuffed $5.1 trillion daily piñata of modern
currencies and unleash a crypto-copia, but the units of account -the monetary
measuring sticks -remained in the world of fiat and gold. This is why you still
see values of cryptocurrency surging and collapsing to this day. Until wall
street and main street overcome the key flaw of all these currencies, that
won’t change, but the real miracle, the real innovation isn’t the financial one.
(07:40)
It’s the underlying technology. This is the blueprint that’s going to give us the
brand new infrastructures and it’s going to do so much more than disrupt
our money or today’s internet. As you’ll see in Life After Google, this new
system will give us new ways to escape the swamp of over-regulation, cut out
intermediaries and rapidly lower costs for doing business. It’s going to help us
free up capital, give power to legions of new entrepreneurs and freely expand
wealth creation. And all of this with real privacy and true identities at it’s core.
In short, this was the internet as it always should have been. So again, if the
problem is that the architecture of the internet is flawed, then the solution is
new architecture based on cryptography. And again, then the promise or the
point of the sales page is to get people to buy the book and get the newsletter
so they can find out about these new companies that are going to replace the
old guards of Google and Facebook and things like that.
(08:33)
And as a result increase in value tremendously and investors who get in early
will realize really large windfalls. So, I wanted to share this one especially
cause we did an olive oil one that was for a consumer product. We did a fungus
one that’s kind of like a health product. But here you have a mechanism for a
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 11 --

7	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Financial
financial product when someone is selling a book and a newsletter and so on,
investment stuff. And that’s an important point because, like I’ve been saying,
really everything, you can do a unique mechanism for everything in a pitch,
in a presentation in a a sales conversation where you’re selling a service,
whatever you’re doing by differentiating, by showing that you understand the
problem and then showing them how you understand better than the prospect
does, in fact that you can tell them about the problem and that here’s the 1%
you were missing. And then by showing that whatever you’re selling, whether
it’s a product or a service, is the solution to that missing 1%, then you can
have a much higher probability of closing that prospect and making the sale,
whether that’s through digital marketing or in person marketing or somewhere
in between. So hopefully that all makes sense. I think those are three great
examples. And we’re going to move on to B of the RMBC method, which stands
for The Brief.
